p: Here you will find all my favorite recipes, carefully curated for every occasion and mood. Let's start cooking!
Each recipe is tagged with badges to help you quickly find what fits your needs.
Full holiday spreads built for the table — every dish planned, every recipe tested. All meals are dairy-free and gluten-free without sacrificing anything.
A full Christmas feast — herb-seared beef tenderloin, all the sides, and two dessert options.
½ tsp porcini mushroom powder (optional — adds deep umami)
Mix all dry rub ingredients together in a bowl.
Pat tenderloin dry and trim any silver skin.
Remove from fridge 60–90 minutes before cooking.
Rest 15 minutes, then slice and serve.
Roast at 425°F for 20–28 minutes until internal temp reaches 125–130°F.
Rub with olive oil, then coat evenly with the rub.
Sear on all sides in a hot cast iron pan.
½ cup DF cream cheese or cashew cream
Whip coconut cream and cream cheese together until smooth.
Fold in horseradish, Dijon, lemon juice, and seasoning.
Chill at least 1 hour before serving.
This sauce can be made up to 2 days in advance — it actually gets better as the flavors meld.
Add potatoes, water, and salt to the Instant Pot.
Pressure cook on HIGH for 12 minutes; quick release.
Drain, then mash with DF butter, milk, and roasted garlic.
Add beef drippings to a muffin tin and heat in oven until smoking hot.
Whisk flour, milk, eggs, and salt into a smooth batter.
Carefully pour batter into the hot tins, filling halfway.
Bake 20–25 minutes. Do not open the oven.
Opening the oven during baking will cause the Yorkshire puddings to deflate. Set a timer and resist.
2 lbs carrots, peeled and cut on the diagonal
Toss carrots with olive oil, maple syrup, thyme, salt, and pepper.
Spread on a baking sheet and roast 25–30 minutes until caramelized.
Toss sprouts with olive oil, salt, and pepper. Add bacon if using.
While roasting, reduce balsamic, pomegranate juice, maple syrup, and rosemary in a saucepan until syrupy.
Toss roasted sprouts in glaze and return to oven 3–4 minutes.
Toss apple slices with maple syrup, cinnamon, and lemon juice. Spread in a baking dish.
Mix oats, almond flour, brown sugar, and DF butter until crumbly. Spread over apples.
Bake 35–40 minutes until golden and bubbling.
Serve warm with dairy-free ice cream or coconut whipped cream.
Combine Merlot, sugar, cinnamon, star anise, cloves, and orange peel in a deep saucepan.
Bring to a simmer and stir until sugar dissolves.
Add pears and poach 20–25 minutes, turning occasionally, until tender.
Remove pears and reduce the poaching liquid to a syrup.
Serve pears drizzled with the warm syrup.
Pears can be poached 1–2 days ahead and stored in the syrup in the fridge. Gently reheat before serving.
A full Thanksgiving spread. All recipes are dairy-free, gluten-free, and soy-free. Includes a day-of cooking timeline for a 4:00 PM dinner.
½ cup apple juice, apple cider, or water
½ cup brown sugar or coconut sugar
¼ cup Dijon or whole-grain mustard (GF)
Combine all glaze ingredients in a small saucepan.
Foil for covering and lining the pan
Place ham cut-side down on the rack and cover loosely with foil.
Pour ½ cup apple juice or cider into the bottom of the pan.
Preheat oven to 325°F. Line roasting pan with foil and place rack inside.
Remove foil for the last 30 minutes and brush glaze every 10 minutes.
Simmer over medium heat ~5 minutes, stirring until slightly thickened. Set aside.
Tent with foil and rest 10–15 minutes before slicing.
3 lb Yukon Gold or Russet potatoes, peeled and cubed
½ cup oat milk or coconut milk
¼ cup olive oil or dairy-free butter
Add potatoes, water, and salt to the Instant Pot.
Seal lid and pressure cook on HIGH for 10 minutes.
Quick release pressure and drain potatoes.
Return to pot; let steam dissipate 1–2 minutes.
Mash with dairy-free butter and milk until smooth.
Season to taste and keep warm.
2 tbsp olive oil or dairy-free butter
Heat oil in a saucepan over medium heat.
Whisk in GF flour to form a roux; cook 1 minute.
Gradually whisk in broth until smooth.
Add coconut aminos, salt, and pepper.
3 lb yams or sweet potatoes, peeled and cubed
¼ cup maple syrup or brown sugar
¼ cup coconut cream or oat milk
3 tbsp olive oil or dairy-free butter
½ cup chopped pecans or pumpkin seeds (optional)
Boil yams 15–20 minutes until tender; drain.
Mash with sweetener, coconut cream, DF butter, vanilla, spices, and salt.
Spread into a baking dish and top with marshmallows (and nuts if using).
Bake 15–20 minutes until marshmallows are golden.
2 lb Brussels sprouts, trimmed and halved
Toss sprouts in olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Simmer balsamic vinegar and maple syrup 5 minutes to reduce.
Drizzle glaze over sprouts before serving.
Toss carrots with olive oil, herbs, salt, and pepper.
The centerpiece of the meal — steaks, pasta, stroganoff, and more.

Tri-Tip with Chimichurri Sous Vide Tri-Tip with Horseradish Cream Sauce Top Sirloin Steak with Garlic Balsamic Pan Sauce Pork Chops with Mustard Cream Sauce Chef John's Classic Beef Stroganoff Lemon Ricotta Pasta
A bold, smoky spice-rubbed tri-tip paired with a bright, garlicky cilantro chimichurri. Grill or sear — either way this is a showstopper.
2 lbs tri-tip steak (or 1–2 lb sirloin steaks)
Make the rub — mix sesame seeds, smoked paprika, salt, and pepper in a small bowl until combined.
Season the steak — rub the spice mixture all over the steak. Let rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Make the chimichurri — while the steak rests, whisk together cilantro, garlic, red wine vinegar, olive oil, and agave nectar in a bowl. Season with salt to taste. Set aside — it gets better as it sits.

Grill — prepare a grill for medium-high heat (for charcoal: leave one side off; for gas: turn one burner off). Grill the steak over direct heat, turning occasionally, until a meat thermometer inserted in the thickest part reads:
Rest — transfer steak to a cutting board and let rest at least 10 minutes before slicing.
Slice and serve — cut against the grain into thin slices. Spoon chimichurri generously over the top and pass the rest at the table.
Tri-tip has changing grain direction — look carefully before slicing. Cutting against the grain makes it dramatically more tender.
Sear in a very hot cast iron skillet 3–4 minutes per side, then finish in a 400°F oven until the desired temperature is reached. Rest before slicing.

l: Sous Vide Tri-Tip with Horseradish Cream Sauce
p: Vacuum-sealed tri-tip cooked low and slow at 131°F, finished with a hard sear, and served alongside roasted garlic parmesan potatoes and balsamic-glazed Brussels sprouts. A complete dinner that rewards patience.
The tri-tip needs 20–24 hours in the water bath. Start it the day before — you'll sear and rest it just before serving.

l: 🥩 Sous Vide Tri-Tip — Start 4+ Hours Ahead
p: ¼ cup prepared horseradish (adjust to taste)
2 tbsp olive oil or unsalted butter (for searing)
2–3 lb tri-tip steak (see note on cook time)
Bag it — vacuum-seal the steak with rosemary sprigs and crushed garlic. Alternatively, use the water displacement method with a zip-top bag.
Make the sauce — while the steak cooks, mix all horseradish cream sauce ingredients in a bowl. Season with salt and pepper, taste and adjust horseradish. Refrigerate until ready to serve.
Rest — transfer to a cutting board and rest 5–10 minutes.
Sear — remove steak from bag and pat completely dry. Heat a cast iron skillet over high heat until smoking hot. Add butter or oil and sear 1–2 minutes per side until deeply browned.
Season the steak — rub all over with salt, pepper, garlic powder, and smoked paprika.
Slice — cut against the grain and serve with horseradish cream sauce.
Season — toss potatoes with olive oil, garlic, Parmesan, Italian seasoning, salt, and pepper in a large bowl.
Roast — spread in a single layer on a baking sheet. Roast for 25–30 minutes, flipping halfway through, until golden and crispy.
Garnish and serve — sprinkle with fresh parsley and serve hot.
Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste
¼ cup toasted walnuts or pecans (optional)
Season — toss Brussels sprouts with olive oil, salt, and pepper. Spread in a single layer on a baking sheet.
Roast — for 20–25 minutes, stirring halfway through, until tender and golden brown.
Prepare the glaze — in a small bowl, whisk together balsamic vinegar and honey.
Glaze — drizzle glaze over the Brussels sprouts and toss to coat.
Optional garnishes — top with toasted walnuts or pecans and dried cranberries before serving.
Preheat oven; prep potatoes and Brussels sprouts
Pull tri-tip from bath, pat dry, sear, rest, slice
Season and start sous vide tri-tip
The sous vide guarantees edge-to-edge perfect doneness every time. The sear at the end adds the crust. You can't overcook it.
A fairly lean version of the classic — just enough sauce to coat every noodle. Call for more cream if it suits your taste. Satisfying and deeply savory.
2 pounds chuck roast, cut into ½-inch thick strips
½ medium onion, sliced or diced
Season the beef strips generously with salt and pepper on all sides.

Sear the beef — heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat until nearly smoking. Add the beef strips and cook, stirring constantly, for about 7 minutes until the liquid evaporates and the meat is browned. Remove beef from the pan and set aside.

Cook the vegetables — reduce heat to medium. Add butter, mushrooms, and onion to the same pan. Cook and stir until the vegetables are lightly browned, about 7 minutes. Add garlic and cook another 1–2 minutes.
Make the roux — sprinkle flour over the vegetables and stir to coat. Cook for 1–2 minutes.

Build the sauce — stir in half the white wine, scraping the bottom of the pan to release any browned bits. Bring to a simmer and cook until slightly thickened, about 3–4 minutes. Add the remaining wine and the beef broth. Stir to combine, bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for about 1 hour until the beef is tender and the sauce has thickened.
Finish — return beef to the pan and stir in the crème fraîche. Cook 2–3 more minutes. Stir in chives and adjust salt and pepper to taste.
If crème fraîche isn't available, sour cream works perfectly as a 1:1 substitute. Stir it in off the heat to prevent curdling.
The long simmer in Step 5 is what makes chuck roast tender and turns the sauce silky. Don't rush it — 60 minutes of gentle simmering is the move.
Pasta made with spinach, garlic, shallot, lemon, and a simple creamy ricotta sauce. Serve with Parmesan cheese and fresh basil for the perfect pasta dinner.
From Two Peas and Their Pod.
12 oz pasta (rigatoni, penne, or your favorite shape)
½ cup freshly grated Parmesan cheese
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste
Cook the pasta — bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Cook pasta al dente according to package instructions. Before draining, reserve ½ cup of pasta water. Drain and set aside.

Wilt the spinach — while the pasta cooks, heat the olive oil over medium heat in a large skillet. Add the shallot and cook 2 minutes. Add the spinach and cook until wilted, about 2–3 minutes. Add the garlic and cook 1 minute more.
Make the sauce — reduce heat to low. Add the ricotta cheese, lemon zest, lemon juice, and Parmesan to the skillet. Add the reserved pasta water and stir until creamy and smooth.
Toss — add the drained pasta to the skillet and stir until well coated in the ricotta sauce. If the sauce seems thick, add a splash more pasta water.
Taste and adjust — season with salt and black pepper. Add more lemon juice if you want a stronger lemon flavor.
Serve immediately — top with crushed red pepper flakes, extra Parmesan, and a squeeze of lemon if desired.
Don't skip reserving the pasta water — the starch in it is what makes the sauce silky and helps it cling to every noodle.
Add grilled chicken, shrimp, or white beans to make this a more substantial meal. Stir in at the same time as the pasta.

l: Pork Chops with Mustard Cream Sauce
p: Diner staple done right — pan-seared pork chops finished in the oven and draped in a rich, tangy mustard cream sauce. Turns everyday pork chops into something special.
4 (10-oz) bone-in pork chops, about 1 inch thick
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
2 tablespoons red wine vinegar (or sherry vinegar)
½ cup veal stock or low-sodium chicken broth
Season the pork chops generously with salt and pepper on both sides.

Sear — heat 2 tablespoons of the olive oil in a large oven-safe skillet over high heat until almost smoking. Add the pork chops and cook, turning once, until well-browned on both sides, about 4 minutes per side. Transfer the skillet to the oven and roast until cooked through, about 10 minutes (internal temp: 145°F). Transfer chops to a plate and let rest.

Build the sauce — pour off any excess fat from the skillet. Heat the remaining 1 tablespoon olive oil over medium heat. Add the shallot and cook, stirring, for 2 minutes. Add the vinegar and cook until nearly evaporated, about 1 minute. Pour in the stock and cream and bring to a boil. Cook until the sauce is reduced by about half and lightly coats a spoon, about 4 minutes.
Finish — stir in the Dijon mustard and parsley. Season with salt and pepper. Return the pork chops and any accumulated juices to the sauce.
Serve — plate the chops and spoon the mustard cream sauce generously over the top.
A proper hard sear in a very hot pan builds the fond (browned bits) that makes the pan sauce sing. Don't move the chops until they release naturally.
A tablespoon of whole-grain mustard stirred in at the end alongside the Dijon adds a nice pop of texture and a slightly milder heat.

l: Top Sirloin Steak with Garlic Balsamic Pan Sauce
p: A complete guide — marinade, pan sauce, and perfect searing technique — for a top sirloin that punches way above its price point.
1 tsp fresh rosemary, chopped (or ½ tsp dried)
½ cup beef broth or red wine
¼ cup cremini or button mushrooms, sliced
¼ tsp fresh or dried rosemary (optional)
Mix all marinade ingredients in a bowl or zip-top bag.
Add steak, ensuring it's fully coated.
Marinate at least 1 hour — 4–6 hours preferred, max 12 hours.
Remove steak, pat completely dry, and bring to room temperature 20–30 minutes before cooking.
Heat pan over medium-high until smoking hot, about 3–5 minutes.
Add neutral oil. Lay steak flat in the pan (away from you). Don't move it for 2–3 minutes.
Flip and sear the other side another 2–3 minutes.
Lower heat to medium. Add 1 tbsp butter, garlic cloves, and rosemary or thyme if using.
Baste the steak with the melted butter for 1–2 minutes.
Rest the steak 5–10 minutes before slicing against the grain.
Make the sauce in the same skillet right after removing the steak:
Reduce heat to medium. Add 1 tbsp butter and mushrooms. Sauté 2–3 minutes.
Add garlic and sauté 30 seconds more.
Deglaze — add balsamic vinegar and broth or wine, whisk well, scraping up all the browned bits from the pan.
Stir in Dijon mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and rosemary. Simmer 3–4 minutes until slightly reduced.
Off heat, whisk in the remaining 1 tbsp butter to finish and gloss the sauce.
Removing all surface moisture before searing is the single biggest factor in getting a deep, brown crust rather than a gray, steamed exterior. Moisture is the enemy of the Maillard reaction.
Top sirloin is lean and affordable. For best results, choose steaks at least ¾ inch thick — thinner steaks cook too fast to develop a proper crust without overcooking the center.
Fast, satisfying meals that don't cut corners on flavor. Ready when you don't have time to fuss.
Air Fryer Pork Chops Crispy Ranch Chicken Tenders

l: Crispy Parmesan–Panko Air Fryer Pork Chops
p: Boneless, ¼-inch thick pork chops with a crispy golden parmesan-panko crust. Fast, foolproof, and packed with flavor.
½ cup finely grated Parmesan cheese
Preheat air fryer to 400°F (205°C) for 3–5 minutes.
Dry the pork chops completely with paper towels.
Make binder — mix mayonnaise and Dijon mustard until smooth.
Make breading — combine all breading ingredients in a shallow dish and mix well.
Coat each chop with the mayo–Dijon binder, then press firmly into breading on both sides.
Spray the air fryer basket lightly with olive oil. Arrange chops in a single layer and mist the tops.
Air fry 4 minutes. Flip carefully, mist again, and cook 3–4 more minutes until golden brown.
Check temp — pork is done at 145°F internal temperature. Rest 2 minutes before serving.
Use a light mist of oil only — oversaturating causes steaming instead of crisping
If oil smokes or browns too fast, reduce temperature to 390°F
Baked chicken tenders with a crunchy panko crust and punchy ranch flavor. Diabetic-friendly, Kosher, and low-fat — without skimping on crunch.
3 tablespoons Homemade Ranch Dressing Mix (1 oz packet)
Preheat oven to 400°F. Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
Toast the Panko bread crumbs on a baking sheet with a quick spray of cooking spray for 5–7 minutes until lightly golden. Set aside.
In a medium-sized bowl, combine all batter ingredients — mayonnaise, ranch dressing mix, Dijon mustard, paprika, salt, and pepper — and mix well.
Add chicken tenderloins to the bowl and coat well.
Remove chicken from the bowl one at a time and add to the dish of toasted Panko. Keep a parchment paper sheet on the baking sheet for easy cleanup.
Using tongs, dredge each chicken tender through bread crumbs until fully coated. Gently press on bread crumbs to coat. Transfer fully-coated tenders to the parchment-lined baking sheet.
Return chicken to oven and bake for 15 minutes.
Serve with your favorite homemade condiment, or freeze for later.

Let tenders cool completely, then store in the freezer for up to 6 months. When ready to use, place on a parchment-lined baking sheet and bake at 425°F for 20–25 minutes until internal temperature reaches at least 165°F.
3 tablespoons = 1 oz, which is equivalent to one packet.
Warming, satisfying bowls — from weeknight easy to weekend worthy.
Thai Pumpkin Soup with Coconut Milk Instant Pot Butternut Squash Soup

l: Thai Pumpkin Soup with Coconut Milk
p: The ultimate winter warmer. Serve this fragrant soup with a swirl of coconut milk and fresh cilantro for a fantastic spiced vegan pumpkin soup.
1 lemongrass stalk, finely chopped or grated
1 bunch fresh cilantro (roots and stalks finely chopped, leaves reserved)
2 pounds butternut pumpkin (or butternut squash), peeled and chopped

Prep the cilantro — remove the roots and stalky ends from the fresh cilantro. Wash the leaves and dry in a salad spinner or paper towel before roughly chopping. Wash the roots and stalks well to remove any dirt clinging to them. Shake off water and finely chop the stalks and roots — these go in the pot.
Build the base — heat oil in a large saucepan over medium-high. Add the onion and garlic. Cook until the onion becomes translucent.
Add aromatics — stir in the ginger, lemongrass, and chopped cilantro stalks and roots. Cook until the stalks soften.

Cook the squash — add the pumpkin and cook 5 minutes. Pour in the vegetable stock and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low and cook for 30 minutes, or until the pumpkin is completely tender. Remove from heat and allow to cool slightly.
Blend — pour the soup into a blender and blend until smooth. Add most of the chopped cilantro leaves, reserving some for garnish. Blend until the soup is speckled with flecks of green.
Finish with coconut milk — return to the saucepan and stir in the coconut milk. Start with half and add more to taste. Warm gently over low heat — do not boil.
Serve — ladle into bowls and top with reserved cilantro, coconut flakes, and sliced red chili if using.

This recipe works beautifully with butternut squash — feel free to substitute. The article originally references butternut pumpkin, which is the same vegetable known by different names in different countries.
This soup keeps well in the refrigerator for up to 4 days and freezes beautifully for up to 3 months. Add the coconut milk after reheating for best texture.
Get the flavor of a slow-simmered squash soup in half the time using an Instant Pot.
6–8 quart Instant Pot (or other pressure cooker)
3 lbs butternut squash, peeled, seeded, and cut into 2-inch cubes
1 tsp kosher salt, plus more to taste
¼ cup heavy cream (or coconut cream for vegan)
Freshly ground black pepper to taste
Small whole or sliced cremini mushrooms, pan-fried until golden
Sauté — set the Instant Pot to Sauté. Melt the butter, then add onion and cook until softened, about 3–4 minutes. Add garlic and cook 1 minute more.
Pressure cook — add the squash, broth, salt, nutmeg, and cayenne. Seal the lid and cook on High Pressure for 10 minutes. Quick-release when done.
Blend — use an immersion blender directly in the pot, or carefully transfer to a blender in batches, and blend until completely smooth.
Finish — stir in the heavy cream. Taste and adjust salt and pepper.
Serve — ladle into bowls and top with a dollop of sour cream or yogurt, a drizzle of olive oil, and pan-fried mushrooms if using.
For a thinner soup, add more broth a splash at a time after blending. For extra richness, stir in an additional tablespoon of butter just before serving.
Make this on the stovetop: sauté aromatics in a large pot, add squash and broth, bring to a boil, then simmer covered for 30 minutes until the squash is completely tender before blending.
Dishes that hold their own — whether alongside a main or as the whole spread.
Pickled Deviled Eggs with Candied Bacon Esquites (Mexican Street Corn Salad) Thai-Inspired Noodle Salad

l: Pickled Deviled Eggs with Candied Bacon Shards
p: Beet-pickled deviled eggs topped with shards of brown sugar candied bacon. Start the eggs 3 days ahead — the bacon is made day-of.
The eggs need 3 days in the brine. Make the candied bacon the day you serve.
2 cans (15 oz each) sliced beets, drained — reserve the liquid
2 cups beet juice (from the reserved liquid)
Combine beet juice, sugar, vinegar, cloves, salt, pepper, and bay leaves in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, reduce heat, and simmer until sugar is fully dissolved, about 5 minutes.
Drain and discard the brine. Cut each egg in half lengthwise and scoop yolks into a bowl.
Measure 2 cups of beet juice from the reserved liquid.
Mix yolks with mayonnaise, relish, and mustard until smooth. Pipe or spoon filling back into each egg white.
Place beets and peeled eggs in a large bowl. Pour the hot brine over them. Let cool, then refrigerate for 3 days.
Preheat oven to 350°F. Place bacon on a rack set over a foil-lined baking sheet.
Whisk together rice vinegar, maple syrup, and black pepper in a small bowl.
Bake bacon 10 minutes, flip, and bake another 5 minutes.
Brush both sides generously with the vinegar-syrup mixture and return to oven.
Continue basting and baking every 5 minutes until the bacon is deep brown and lacquered, about 20 minutes total.
Let cool completely on the rack — it crisps as it cools.
The bacon needs to be completely cool and set before you break it, or it'll bend instead of shard.
Arrange filled deviled eggs on a serving platter.
Break the cooled candied bacon into rough shards — mix of large and small for texture and height.
Press one or two shards upright into the filling of each egg just before serving.
Once the bacon hits the eggs it will start to soften. Add the shards at the last minute for maximum crunch.
The off-the-cob version of elotes — all the flavor, much easier to eat.
3 cans fire-roasted corn, drained (fresh preferred when in season — cut from 4–6 ears and char in a cast iron skillet)
2 pinches each salt and black pepper
3 tbsp mayonnaise, or more to taste
¼ small jalapeño, seeded and minced

Char the corn — heat a large cast iron skillet over high heat until shimmering. Add drained corn in batches, season with salt and pepper, and cook without stirring until lightly charred, about 3 minutes. Stir once and cook 1 more minute. Set aside.
Make the dressing — in a large bowl, whisk together mayonnaise, ancho chili powder, cayenne, lime juice, garlic, shallots, and jalapeño.
Toss — stir in the charred corn until well coated. Fold in half the cilantro and half the Cotija.
Taste and adjust seasoning. Top with remaining cilantro and Cotija. Serve with lime wedges.
Fire-roasted canned corn is a great shortcut — just drain and pat dry before charring so it crisps instead of steams. Fresh cut corn straight from the cob gives you more sweetness and better char.
Cold soba noodles in a punchy sesame-lime dressing with carrots and peanuts. Gets better the longer it sits.
2 tsp red pepper flakes, or to taste
¼ cup coarsely chopped salted peanuts
Cook soba noodles per package directions. Drain and rinse thoroughly with cold water. Set aside.
Whisk together sesame oil, rice vinegar, soy sauce, lime zest and juice, brown sugar, garlic, and red pepper flakes until the sugar dissolves.
Cut noodles into roughly 3-inch lengths and toss into the dressing. Cover and refrigerate at least 1 hour.
Before serving, toss again. If it seems dry, splash with a little extra soy sauce and rice vinegar. Top with carrots, cilantro, and peanuts.
Skip the pepper flakes, garlic, and brown sugar — add ¼ cup toasted sesame seeds instead for a cleaner, more delicate flavor.
Ramen noodles work as a substitute for soba in a pinch.
Recipes worth raising a glass to.
Get all the flavors of a great Bloody Mary without mixers — infused right into the vodka. Pro tip: use high quality vodka.
By Emily Farris and Jeff Akin, co-founders of Feed Me Creative.
Sous vide precision cooker (Anova or similar)
Large zip-top bag or vacuum sealer
1 anaheim pepper, stemmed, seeds removed, sliced into ½-inch pieces
¼ onion, peeled and sliced into ½-inch pieces
1 jalapeño pepper, sliced in half, seeds removed
Set the Anova Sous Vide Precision Cooker to 145°F (62.8°C).
Bag it — combine all ingredients in a large zip-top bag. Seal the bag using the water immersion technique.
Strain — when the timer goes off, remove the bag from the water bath. Strain the contents through a fine-mesh strainer into a large bowl. Let cool to room temperature.
Bottle — transfer to an airtight storage bottle and refrigerate overnight before serving.
Store in an airtight bottle in the refrigerator for up to six months. Shake or stir before using.
Use in place of plain vodka in any Bloody Mary recipe. The tomato, pepper, and lime are already in the spirit — just add Bloody Mary mix or tomato juice and garnish as usual.
